**Scottish independence** ([Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_language "Scottish Gaelic language"): *Neo-eisimeileachd na h-Alba*; [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"): *Scots unthirldom*)[[1]](#cite_note-1) is the [political movement](/wiki/Political_movement "Political movement") advocating for [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") to become a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state"), independent from the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Scotland [was an independent kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland") through the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), and fought [wars to maintain its independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence") from the [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England"). The two kingdoms were united in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") in 1603 when, upon the death of [Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_I "Queen Elizabeth I") of England, [King James VI](/wiki/King_James_VI "King James VI") of Scotland also became James I of England. The kingdoms were united politically into one kingdom called [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") by the [Acts of Union 1707](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") during the reign of [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain").[[6]](#cite_note-6) This united the countries, ended the wars of independence and created relative peace. Political campaigns for Scottish self-government began in the 19th century, initially in the form of demands for [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule") within the United Kingdom. Two referendums on [devolution](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom") were held [in 1979](/wiki/1979_Scottish_devolution_referendum "1979 Scottish devolution referendum") and [in 1997](/wiki/1997_Scottish_devolution_referendum "1997 Scottish devolution referendum"), and a devolved [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") was established on 1 July 1999.

The pro-independence [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) first became the governing party of the devolved parliament following the [2007 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2007_Scottish_Parliament_election "2007 Scottish Parliament election"), and it won an outright majority of seats at the [2011 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2011_Scottish_Parliament_election "2011 Scottish Parliament election"). This led to [an agreement](/wiki/Edinburgh_Agreement_(2012) "Edinburgh Agreement (2012)") between the [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") and [British governments](/wiki/British_government "British government") to hold the [2014 Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum"). Voters were asked: "Should Scotland be an independent country?"[[7]](#cite_note-7) 44.7 per cent answered "Yes" and 55.3 per cent answered "No". There was a record [voter turnout](/wiki/Voter_turnout "Voter turnout") of 85 per cent.[[8]](#cite_note-No-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Now-9)

A [second referendum on independence](/wiki/Proposed_second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Proposed second Scottish independence referendum") has been proposed, particularly since the UK [voted to leave](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") in the [June 2016 membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") and since pro-independence parties increased their majority at the [2021 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2021_Scottish_Parliament_election "2021 Scottish Parliament election"). In June 2022, [Nicola Sturgeon](/wiki/Nicola_Sturgeon "Nicola Sturgeon"), the [first minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland") and the [leader of the SNP](/wiki/Leader_of_the_SNP "Leader of the SNP"), proposed the date of 19 October 2023 for a new independence referendum, subject to confirmation of its legality and [constitutionality](/wiki/Constitutionality "Constitutionality").[[10]](#cite_note-10) In November 2022 the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") ruled that the Scottish Parliament did not have the power to legislate for a second referendum.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Kingdom of Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Kingdom of Scotland")]

Main article: [Kingdom of Scotland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Scotland "Kingdom of Scotland")

Scotland emerged as an independent [polity](/wiki/Polity "Polity") during the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages") with some historians dating its foundation from the reign of [Kenneth MacAlpin](/wiki/Kenneth_MacAlpin "Kenneth MacAlpin") in 843.[[12]](#cite_note-Marr10-12)[[a]](#cite_note-13) The level of independence of the Scottish kingdom was fought over by the [Scottish kings](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_monarchs "List of Scottish monarchs") and by the [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") and [Angevin](/wiki/Angevin_Empire "Angevin Empire") rulers of England who petitioned the [pope](/wiki/Bishop_of_Rome "Bishop of Rome") in Rome and other foreign rulers.[[12]](#cite_note-Marr10-12)

A watershed in the Scottish kingdom's history was a succession crisis that erupted in 1290 when [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") claimed the right of appointment to the Scottish throne. The [Auld Alliance](/wiki/Auld_Alliance "Auld Alliance") of Scotland and France against English interests was first invoked at that time and remained active through to the 16th century. The [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence") ended in a renewed kingdom under [Robert the Bruce](/wiki/Robert_the_Bruce "Robert the Bruce") (crowned 1306), whose grandson [Robert II of Scotland](/wiki/Robert_II_of_Scotland "Robert II of Scotland") was the first Scottish king of the [House of Stuart](/wiki/House_of_Stuart "House of Stuart").

### Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Union")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Houghton_STC_13011_-_Hayward%2C_Treatise.jpg/250px-Houghton_STC_13011_-_Hayward%2C_Treatise.jpg)](/wiki/File:Houghton_STC_13011_-_Hayward,_Treatise.jpg)

*A treatise of union of the two realmes of England and Scotland* by the English historian [Sir John Hayward](/wiki/John_Hayward_(historian) "John Hayward (historian)"), 1604

From 1603 Scotland and England shared the same monarch in a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") when [James VI of Scotland](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") was declared King of England and Scotland in what was known as the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns"). After [James II and VII](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") was [deposed in 1688](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") amid Catholic-Protestant disputes, and as the line of Protestant Stuarts showed signs of failing (as indeed occurred in 1714), English fears that Scotland would select a different monarch, potentially causing conflict within Great Britain, and the bankruptcy of many Scottish nobles through the [Darien scheme](/wiki/Darien_scheme "Darien scheme") led to the formal union of the two kingdoms in 1707, with the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") and subsequent [Acts of Union](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1707 "Act of Union 1707"), to form the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). Scottish [Jacobite](/wiki/Jacobitism "Jacobitism") resistance to the union, led by descendants of James II and VII including [Bonnie Prince Charlie](/wiki/Bonnie_Prince_Charlie "Bonnie Prince Charlie"), continued until 1746.

The [United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") was formed by the [Acts of Union 1800](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1800 "Acts of Union 1800"), which united the Kingdom of Great Britain and the [Kingdom of Ireland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ireland "Kingdom of Ireland"). Following the [Irish War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence") (1919–21) and the [Anglo-Irish Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Irish_Treaty "Anglo-Irish Treaty") that ended the war, Ireland was [partitioned](/wiki/Partition_of_Ireland "Partition of Ireland") into two states: [Southern Ireland](/wiki/Southern_Ireland_(1921%E2%80%9322) "Southern Ireland (1921–22)"), which opted to become independent (and is now known as Republic of Ireland), and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland"), which – given its geographical extent which tended to ensure a Unionist majority – chose to remain within the United Kingdom.

### Home rule movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Home rule movement")]

Main article: [Scottish Assembly](/wiki/Scottish_Assembly "Scottish Assembly")

The "Home Rule" movement for a Scottish Assembly was first taken up in 1853 by the [National Association for the Vindication of Scottish Rights](/wiki/National_Association_for_the_Vindication_of_Scottish_Rights "National Association for the Vindication of Scottish Rights"), a body close to the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"). A key element in this movement was the comparison with Ireland. The original movement broadened its political appeal and soon began to receive [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)") backing.[[13]](#cite_note-bbchistory-14)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In 1885, the post of [Secretary for Scotland](/wiki/Secretary_for_Scotland "Secretary for Scotland") and the [Scottish Office](/wiki/Scottish_Office "Scottish Office") were re-established to promote Scotland's interests and express its concerns to the UK Parliament. In 1886, however, Liberal Prime Minister [William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone") introduced the [Irish Home Rule Bill](/wiki/Irish_Government_Bill_1886 "Irish Government Bill 1886"). It was not regarded as an immediate constitutional priority however, particularly when the Irish Home Rule Bill was defeated in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom").

Immediately before the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), the [Liberal Government](/wiki/Liberal_Government_1905%E2%80%9315 "Liberal Government 1905–15") led by [H. H. Asquith](/wiki/H._H._Asquith "H. H. Asquith") supported the concept of "Home Rule all round", whereby Scottish home rule would follow the Irish home rule proposed in the [Government of Ireland Act 1914](/wiki/Government_of_Ireland_Act_1914 "Government of Ireland Act 1914").[[14]](#cite_note-Marr1-15) Asquith believed that there was an iniquity in that the component parts of the United Kingdom could come together to act together in common purposes, but those components could not deal with internal matters that did not require consent across the UK.[[14]](#cite_note-Marr1-15) This was not a nationalist philosophy, but instead Asquith was acting in the belief that federalism was the "true basis of union" and that centralising power in Westminster was a political blunder.[[15]](#cite_note-Marr2-16) A Scottish Home Rule bill was first presented to Parliament in 1913, but its progress was soon ended as Parliament focused on emergency measures necessitated by the First World War.[[15]](#cite_note-Marr2-16)

Unlike Ireland, which rebelled in the [Easter Rising](/wiki/Easter_Rising "Easter Rising") and fought a [War of Independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence"), Scotland did not resist central rule.[[15]](#cite_note-Marr2-16) There was, however, a persistent demand for Scottish home rule.[[15]](#cite_note-Marr2-16) The Scottish Office was relocated to [St Andrew's House](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_House "St Andrew's House") in Edinburgh during the 1930s.[[13]](#cite_note-bbchistory-14)[[16]](#cite_note-17) The [Scottish Covenant](/wiki/Scottish_Covenant "Scottish Covenant") was a petition to the UK Government asking for home rule. It was first proposed in 1930 by [John MacCormick](/wiki/John_MacCormick "John MacCormick") and formally written in 1949. The petition "was eventually signed by two million people"[[17]](#cite_note-1999BBC-18) (the population of Scotland was recorded as 5,100,000 in the [1951 UK Census](/wiki/Census_in_the_United_Kingdom "Census in the United Kingdom")[[18]](#cite_note-19)). The covenant was ignored by the main political parties.[[17]](#cite_note-1999BBC-18) In 1950 the [Stone of Destiny](/wiki/Stone_of_Scone "Stone of Scone") was removed from Westminster Abbey by a group of Scottish nationalist students.

The question of full independence, or the less controversial [home rule](/wiki/Home_rule "Home rule"), did not re-enter the political mainstream until 1960, after the famous [Wind of Change](/wiki/Wind_of_Change_(speech) "Wind of Change (speech)") speech by Conservative Prime Minister [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan"). This speech marked the start of a rapid [decolonisation](/wiki/Decolonisation "Decolonisation") in Africa and the end of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). The UK had already suffered the international humiliation of the 1956 [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis"), which showed that it was no longer the superpower it had been before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). For many in Scotland, this served to undermine one of the principal [raisons d'être](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/raison_d%27%C3%AAtre "wikt:raison d'être") for the United Kingdom and also symbolised the end of popular [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") and the Imperial unity that had united the then-prominent [Scottish Unionist Party](/wiki/Unionist_Party_(Scotland) "Unionist Party (Scotland)"). The Unionist Party subsequently suffered a steady decline in support.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21)

### 1979 First devolution referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1979 First devolution referendum")]

Main article: [1979 Scottish devolution referendum](/wiki/1979_Scottish_devolution_referendum "1979 Scottish devolution referendum")

The [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) won their second-ever seat in the House of Commons in 1967, when [Winnie Ewing](/wiki/Winnie_Ewing "Winnie Ewing") was the unexpected winner of the [1967 Hamilton by-election](/wiki/1967_Hamilton_by-election "1967 Hamilton by-election"). The seat was previously a safe Labour Party seat, and this victory brought the SNP to national prominence, leading to [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath")'s 1968 [Declaration of Perth](/wiki/Declaration_of_Perth "Declaration of Perth") and the establishment of the [Kilbrandon Commission](/wiki/Royal_Commission_on_the_Constitution_(United_Kingdom) "Royal Commission on the Constitution (United Kingdom)").[[21]](#cite_note-22) The discovery of [North Sea oil](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil") off the east coast of Scotland in 1970 further invigorated the debate over Scottish independence.[[22]](#cite_note-bbchistory2-23) The SNP organised a hugely successful campaign entitled "[It's Scotland's oil](/wiki/It%27s_Scotland%27s_oil "It's Scotland's oil")", emphasising how the discovery of oil could benefit Scotland's struggling [deindustrialising](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization") economy and its populace.[[23]](#cite_note-McCrone-24) At the [February 1974 general election](/wiki/February_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "February 1974 United Kingdom general election"), seven SNP MPs were elected. The general election resulted in a hung parliament, so Prime Minister [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson") called a second election for [October 1974](/wiki/October_1974_United_Kingdom_general_election "October 1974 United Kingdom general election"), when the SNP performed even better than in February, winning 11 seats and obtaining over 30% of the total vote in Scotland.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

In January 1974, the Conservative government had commissioned the [McCrone report](/wiki/McCrone_report "McCrone report"), written by Professor Gavin McCrone, a leading government economist, to report on the viability of an independent Scotland. He concluded that oil would have given an independent Scotland one of the strongest currencies in Europe. The report went on to say that officials advised government ministers on how to take "the wind out of the SNP sails". A common myth regarding the report is that when handed over to the incoming Labour government and classified as secret because of Labour fears over the surge in Scottish National Party popularity, the document came to light only in 2005, when the SNP obtained the report under the [Freedom of Information Act 2000](/wiki/Freedom_of_Information_Act_2000 "Freedom of Information Act 2000").[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27) McCrone himself has rejected this claim.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

The [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), led by Harold Wilson, won the October 1974 general election with the very narrow majority of only three seats. Following their election to Parliament, the SNP MPs pressed for the creation of a Scottish Assembly: a viewpoint which was given added credibility by the conclusions of the Kilbrandon Commission. However, opponents demanded that a referendum be held on the issue. Although the Labour Party and the Scottish National Party both officially supported [devolution](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom"), support was split in both parties. Labour was divided between those who favoured devolution and those who wanted to maintain a full central Westminster government. In the SNP, there was division between those who saw devolution as a stepping stone to independence and those who feared it might detract from that ultimate goal.[[22]](#cite_note-bbchistory2-23) The resignation of Harold Wilson from office in 1976 brought [James Callaghan](/wiki/James_Callaghan "James Callaghan") to power, but his small majority was eroded by several by-election losses, and the government became increasingly unpopular. Deals were made with the SNP and [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") to hold referendums on devolution in exchange for their support, helping to prolong the government's life.

The result of the referendum in Scotland was a narrow majority in favour of devolution (52% to 48%),[[22]](#cite_note-bbchistory2-23) but a condition of the referendum was that 40% of the total electorate should vote in favour in order to make it valid. But the turnout was only 63.6%, so only 32.9% of the electorate voted "Yes". The [Scotland Act 1978](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1978 "Scotland Act 1978") was consequently repealed in March 1979 by a vote of 301–206 in Parliament. In the wake of the referendum, the supporters of the bill conducted a protest campaign under the slogan "Scotland said yes". They said that the 40% rule was undemocratic and that the referendum results justified the establishment of the assembly. Campaigners for a "No" vote countered that voters had been told before the referendum that failing to vote was as good as a "No".[[28]](#cite_note-29) It was therefore incorrect to conclude that the relatively low turnout was entirely due to [voter apathy](/wiki/Voter_fatigue "Voter fatigue").

In protest, the SNP withdrew their support from the government. A [motion of no confidence](/wiki/Motion_of_no_confidence "Motion of no confidence") was then tabled by the Conservatives and supported by the SNP, the Liberals and [Ulster Unionists](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party"). It passed by one vote on 28 March 1979, forcing the [May 1979 general election](/wiki/1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "1979 United Kingdom general election"), which was won by the Conservatives led by [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher"). Prime Minister Callaghan described the decision of the SNP to bring down the Labour government as "[turkeys voting for Christmas](/wiki/Turkeys_voting_for_Christmas "Turkeys voting for Christmas")".[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31) The SNP group was reduced from 11 MPs to 2 at the 1979 general election, while devolution was opposed by the Conservative governments led by Margaret Thatcher and [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major").

### 1997 Second devolution referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1997 Second devolution referendum")]

Main article: [1997 Scottish devolution referendum](/wiki/1997_Scottish_devolution_referendum "1997 Scottish devolution referendum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Debating_chamber%2C_Scottish_Parliament_%2831-05-2006%29.jpg/330px-Debating_chamber%2C_Scottish_Parliament_%2831-05-2006%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Debating_chamber,_Scottish_Parliament_(31-05-2006).jpg)

The [Debating](/wiki/Debating "Debating") chamber of the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), commonly known as Holyrood.

Supporters of Scottish independence continued to hold mixed views on the [Home Rule](/wiki/Scottish_Assembly "Scottish Assembly") movement, which included many supporters of union who wanted [devolution](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") within the framework of the United Kingdom. Some saw it as a stepping stone to independence, while others wanted to go straight for independence.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In the years of the Conservative government after 1979, the [Campaign for a Scottish Assembly](/wiki/Campaign_for_a_Scottish_Assembly "Campaign for a Scottish Assembly") was established, eventually publishing the [Claim of Right 1989](/wiki/Claim_of_Right_1989 "Claim of Right 1989"). This led to the [Scottish Constitutional Convention](/wiki/Scottish_Constitutional_Convention "Scottish Constitutional Convention"). The convention promoted consensus on devolution on a cross-party basis, though the Conservative Party refused to co-operate and the Scottish National Party withdrew from the discussions when it became clear that the convention was unwilling to discuss Scottish independence as a constitutional option.[[22]](#cite_note-bbchistory2-23) Arguments against devolution and the Scottish Parliament, levelled mainly by the Conservative Party, were that the Parliament would create a "slippery slope" to Scottish independence and provide the pro-independence Scottish National Party with a route to government.[[32]](#cite_note-Devolution-33) Prime Minister [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major") campaigned during the [1997 general election](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election") on the slogan "72 hours to save the union". His party ultimately suffered the worst electoral defeat in 91 years.[[33]](#cite_note-Major-34)

The Labour Party won the 1997 general election in a landslide, and [Donald Dewar](/wiki/Donald_Dewar "Donald Dewar") as [Secretary of State for Scotland](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Scotland "Secretary of State for Scotland") agreed to the proposals for a [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"). A referendum was held in September and 74.3% of those who voted approved the devolution plan (44.87% of the electorate).[[34]](#cite_note-35) The [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") subsequently approved the [Scotland Act 1998](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998") which created an elected Scottish Parliament with control over most domestic policy.[[22]](#cite_note-bbchistory2-23) In May 1999, Scotland held its [first election](/wiki/1999_Scottish_Parliament_election "1999 Scottish Parliament election") for a devolved parliament, and in July 1999, the Scottish Parliament held session for the first time since the previous parliament had been adjourned in 1707, after a gap of 292 years. Donald Dewar of the Labour Party subsequently became the [First Minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), while the Scottish National Party became the main opposition party. The egalitarian song "[A Man's A Man for A' That](/wiki/A_Man%27s_A_Man_for_A%27_That "A Man's A Man for A' That")", by [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns"), was performed at the opening ceremony.[[35]](#cite_note-36)

The Scottish Parliament is a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") legislature comprising 129 [members](/wiki/Members_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Members of the Scottish Parliament"). 73 members (57 pc) represent individual [constituencies](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament_constituencies "Scottish Parliament constituencies") and are elected on a [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post") system. 56 members (43 pc) are elected in eight different electoral regions by the [additional member system](/wiki/Additional_member_system_(Scottish_Parliament) "Additional member system (Scottish Parliament)"). Members serve for a four-year term. The monarch appoints one [Member of the Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament"), on the nomination of the Parliament, to be [First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), with the convention being that the leader of the party with the largest number of seats is appointed First Minister, although any member who can command the confidence of a majority of the chamber could conceivably be appointed First Minister. All other Ministers are appointed and dismissed by the First Minister, and together they make up the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), the executive arm of government.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

The Scottish Parliament has [legislative](/wiki/Legislative "Legislative") authority for all non-[reserved matters](/wiki/Reserved_and_excepted_matters "Reserved and excepted matters") relating to Scotland, and has a limited power to vary income tax, nicknamed the [Tartan Tax](/wiki/Tartan_Tax "Tartan Tax"), a power it did not exercise and which was later replaced by wider tax-varying powers. The Scottish Parliament can refer devolved matters back to Westminster to be considered as part of United Kingdom-wide legislation by passing a [Legislative Consent Motion](/wiki/Legislative_Consent_Motion "Legislative Consent Motion") if United Kingdom-wide legislation is considered to be more appropriate for certain issues. The programmes of legislation enacted by the Scottish Parliament since 1999 have seen a divergence in the provision of [public services](/wiki/Public_services "Public services") compared to the rest of the United Kingdom. For instance, the costs of a university education, and care services for the elderly are free at point of use in Scotland, while fees are paid in the rest of the UK. Scotland was the first country in the UK to ban smoking in enclosed public places in March 2006.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### 2014 independence referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2014 independence referendum")]

Main article: [2014 Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Scottish_independence_importance_graph_2011.png/330px-Scottish_independence_importance_graph_2011.png)](/wiki/File:Scottish_independence_importance_graph_2011.png)

Survey of the importance of holding a referendum, carried out by the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") in April 2011.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ad/Scotland_Independence_Referendum_Result_2014.png)](/wiki/File:Scotland_Independence_Referendum_Result_2014.png)

Referendum result

In its manifesto for the [2007 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2007_Scottish_Parliament_election "2007 Scottish Parliament election"), the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") (SNP) pledged to hold an independence [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") by 2010.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) After winning the election,[[40]](#cite_note-41) the SNP-controlled Scottish Government published a [white paper](/wiki/White_paper "White paper") entitled "[Choosing Scotland's Future](/wiki/Choosing_Scotland%27s_Future "Choosing Scotland's Future")", which outlined options for the future of Scotland, including independence.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) Scottish Labour, the [Scottish Conservatives](/wiki/Scottish_Conservatives "Scottish Conservatives") and [Scottish Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats") opposed a referendum offering independence as an option. Prime Minister [Gordon Brown](/wiki/Gordon_Brown "Gordon Brown") also publicly attacked the independence option.[[43]](#cite_note-44) The three main parties opposed to independence instead formed a [Commission on Scottish Devolution](/wiki/Commission_on_Scottish_Devolution "Commission on Scottish Devolution"), chaired by [Kenneth Calman](/wiki/Kenneth_Calman "Kenneth Calman").[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46) This reviewed devolution and considered all constitutional options apart from independence.[[46]](#cite_note-47) In August 2009, the Scottish Government announced that the [Referendum (Scotland) Bill, 2010](/wiki/Referendum_(Scotland)_Bill,_2010 "Referendum (Scotland) Bill, 2010"), which would detail the question and conduct of a possible referendum on the issue of independence, would be part of its legislative programme for 2009–10. The Bill was not expected to be passed, because of the SNP's status as a minority government and the opposition of all other major parties in Parliament.[[47]](#cite_note-ReferendumBill2010-48)[[48]](#cite_note-Times3Sep09-49) In September 2010, the Scottish Government announced that no referendum would occur before the 2011 Scottish Parliament election.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

The SNP won an overall majority in the Scottish Parliament at the [2011 Scottish election](/wiki/2011_Scottish_Parliament_election "2011 Scottish Parliament election").[[50]](#cite_note-51)[[51]](#cite_note-52) First Minister [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond") stated his desire to hold a referendum "in the second half of the parliament", which would place it in 2014 or 2015.[[52]](#cite_note-53) In January 2012, the UK Government offered to provide the Scottish Parliament with the specific powers to hold a referendum, providing it was "fair, legal and decisive".[[53]](#cite_note-contrary-54)[[54]](#cite_note-chaos-55) Negotiations continued between the two governments until October 2012, when the [Edinburgh Agreement](/wiki/Edinburgh_Agreement_(2012) "Edinburgh Agreement (2012)") was reached.[[55]](#cite_note-timeline-56) The Scottish Independence Referendum (Franchise) Act 2013 was passed by the Scottish Parliament on 27 June 2013 and received Royal Assent on 7 August 2013.[[56]](#cite_note-57) On 15 November 2013, the Scottish Government published *[Scotland's Future](/wiki/Scotland%27s_Future "Scotland's Future")*, a 670-page white paper laying out the case for independence and the means through which Scotland might become an independent country.[[57]](#cite_note-SF_Reaction-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/David_Cameron_in_Edinburgh_%2816342998225%29.jpg/250px-David_Cameron_in_Edinburgh_%2816342998225%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:David_Cameron_in_Edinburgh_(16342998225).jpg)

UK Prime Minister David Cameron in Edinburgh to discuss the new powers that Scotland would obtain through the [Scotland Act 2016](/wiki/Scotland_Act_2016 "Scotland Act 2016")

After a protracted period of negotiation, a public debate between Salmond and [Better Together](/wiki/Better_Together_(campaign) "Better Together (campaign)") leader [Alistair Darling](/wiki/Alistair_Darling "Alistair Darling") was arranged.[[58]](#cite_note-59) On the morning before the televised debate, a joint statement, pledging greater devolved powers to Scotland in the event of a "No" vote, was signed by Prime Minister [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron") (Leader of the Conservative Party), Deputy Prime Minister [Nick Clegg](/wiki/Nick_Clegg "Nick Clegg") (Leader of the Liberal Democrats), and Leader of the Opposition [Ed Miliband](/wiki/Ed_Miliband "Ed Miliband") (Leader of the Labour Party).[[59]](#cite_note-Sev-60)

The BBC website announced the final result of the referendum at 06:24 on 19 September 2014: the "No" vote prevailed with 55% (2,001,926) of the votes from an overall voter turnout of 84.5%. Chief counting officer Mary Pitcaithly stated: "It is clear that the majority of people voting have voted No to the referendum question." The "Yes" vote received 45% (1,617,989) support—the winning total needed was 1,852,828. Results were compiled from 32 council areas, with Glasgow backing independence—voting 53.5% "Yes" to 46.5% "No" (turnout in the area was 75%)—and Edinburgh voting against independence by 61% to 39% (turnout in the area was 84%). Darling stated in his post-result speech, "The silent have spoken", while Salmond stated, "I accept the verdict of the people, and I call on all of Scotland to follow suit in accepting the democratic verdict".[[8]](#cite_note-No-8)[[60]](#cite_note-Wit-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

### UK withdrawal from the European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: UK withdrawal from the European Union")]

See also: [Proposed second Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/Proposed_second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Proposed second Scottish independence referendum"), [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), and [2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/UK_and_Scottish_ministers_in_Brexit_talks_%2836067878280%29.jpg/250px-UK_and_Scottish_ministers_in_Brexit_talks_%2836067878280%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:UK_and_Scottish_ministers_in_Brexit_talks_(36067878280).jpg)

Ministers from the Scottish and UK Governments meet to discuss Brexit, 2017

In 2016, the UK voted to leave the EU in the Brexit referendum, however Scotland voted to remain by 62% to 38%.[[64]](#cite_note-BBCUKEUREF-65) Leading pro-independence figures suggested a second independence referendum.[[65]](#cite_note-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67) For example, [First Minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland") [Nicola Sturgeon](/wiki/Nicola_Sturgeon "Nicola Sturgeon") said that she was looking at all options to "secure our place in the EU", and that a second referendum was "highly likely".[[67]](#cite_note-BBC2462016-68) However, a spokesperson for Prime Minister [Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May") said that "The prime minister and the government does not believe that there is a mandate for [a second referendum]. There was one only two years ago. There was an extremely high turnout and there was a resounding result in favour of Scotland remaining in the UK".[[68]](#cite_note-69)[[69]](#cite_note-70)

At the [2019 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election"), the SNP won 48 out of 59 Scottish seats. Sturgeon asked Prime Minister [Boris Johnson](/wiki/Boris_Johnson "Boris Johnson") for his consent to hold another referendum.[[70]](#cite_note-71) However, Johnson declined her request. He said that Sturgeon and her predecessor Alex Salmond had promised that the 2014 referendum would be a "once in a generation" vote.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

Shortly before the UK left the [European single market](/wiki/European_single_market "European single market"), the Boris Johnson regime sought through the [United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Internal_Market_Act_2020 "United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020") to restrict the legislative competence of the Scottish Parliament. The primary purpose of the act is to constrain the capacity of the devolved institutions to use their regulatory autonomy.[[72]](#cite_note-DouHu22-73) The legislation undermines the capability of the Scottish legislature to make different economic or social choices from those made in Westminster.[[81]](#cite_note-UKIM-82) In a January 2021 editorial concerning rising support for independence and its [potential to break up the union](/wiki/Potential_breakup_of_the_United_Kingdom "Potential breakup of the United Kingdom"), the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")* indicates that the Internal Market Act may serve to further the cause of independence:[[82]](#cite_note-BattleFT21-83)

> An example of what not to do was the government’s Internal Market Act, in which London retook control of structural funds previously disbursed by the EU.

This view was mirrored by the Scottish Government in a report published in March 2021, which states that the act is "radically undermining the powers and democratic accountability of the Scottish Parliament."[[83]](#cite_note-AfterBr21-84)

### 2021 Scottish elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2021 Scottish elections")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Agreement_with_Scottish_Green_Party_at_Bute.jpg/250px-Agreement_with_Scottish_Green_Party_at_Bute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agreement_with_Scottish_Green_Party_at_Bute.jpg)

Following the 2021 election, the SNP and [Scottish Greens](/wiki/Scottish_Greens "Scottish Greens") agreed a power sharing agreement known as the [Bute House Agreement](/wiki/Bute_House_Agreement "Bute House Agreement"), giving the SNP government a majority in the Scottish Parliament

In January 2021, Nicola Sturgeon said that another referendum would be held if pro-independence parties won a majority of seats at the [2021 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2021_Scottish_Parliament_election "2021 Scottish Parliament election"). Opposition parties criticised the SNP, stating that they were putting independence ahead of the ongoing [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Scotland "COVID-19 pandemic in Scotland").[[84]](#cite_note-85) Although the SNP fell one seat short of winning outright, the eight seats won by the Scottish Greens meant that pro-independence parties had won a majority of seats in the election.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Speaking after the election, both SNP and Conservative representatives said that a referendum would not occur during the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

The Scottish Government plans to hold a second independence referendum before the end of the current Scottish Parliament.[[87]](#cite_note-:4-88) Nicola Sturgeon has indicated the vote would be held by 2023. Boris Johnson has stated that he would not grant authorisation for a referendum, but the SNP has indicated it is prepared to hold a referendum regardless. According to a poll of 1000 voters conducted for *[Politico Europe](/wiki/Politico_Europe "Politico Europe")*, 43% said they agreed that Scotland should only hold a second independence referendum if the U.K. government agrees to it.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

In January 2022, *[The Herald](/wiki/The_Herald_(Glasgow) "The Herald (Glasgow)")* reported that the Scottish Government were paying eleven civil servants a total of £700,000 per year to plan for Scottish independence. In its response to the newspaper, the government stated "As set out in the 2021/22 Programme for Government (PfG), the Scottish Government will work to ensure that a legitimate and constitutional referendum can be held within this Parliament, and if the Covid crisis is over, within the first half of this Parliament." The [Scottish Conservatives](/wiki/Scottish_Conservatives "Scottish Conservatives") constitution spokesperson [Donald Cameron](/wiki/Donald_Cameron_(Scottish_politician) "Donald Cameron (Scottish politician)") and [Scottish Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats") leader [Alex Cole-Hamilton](/wiki/Alex_Cole-Hamilton "Alex Cole-Hamilton") opposed the spending. Cole-Hamilton said "to spend more than £500,000 on a fool's errand of another prospectus for independence makes it less of a white paper and more of a white elephant" and suggested the SNP and Green Party are "off their rockers". SNP President Michael Russell remarked that the SNP and Green Party's victory in the election indicated they have a mandate to hold a second referendum to make Scotland an independent country.[[87]](#cite_note-:4-88)

### Proposed 2023 independence referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Proposed 2023 independence referendum")]

Main article: [Proposed second Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/Proposed_second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Proposed second Scottish independence referendum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Scottish_Government_Press_Conference_-_Renewing_Democracy_through_Independence_-_14th_July_2022_%2852215837243%29.jpg/250px-Scottish_Government_Press_Conference_-_Renewing_Democracy_through_Independence_-_14th_July_2022_%2852215837243%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottish_Government_Press_Conference_-_Renewing_Democracy_through_Independence_-_14th_July_2022_(52215837243).jpg)

The Scottish Government began publishing independence prospectus papers, *[Building a New Scotland](/wiki/Building_a_New_Scotland "Building a New Scotland")*, under First Minister Nicola Sturgeon in 2022

On 28 June 2022, First Minister Nicola Sturgeon proposed to hold a second Scottish independence referendum on 19 October 2023, provided that the legality and constitutionality of the referendum is guaranteed.[[89]](#cite_note-19_october-90) She set out a three-stage process, starting with the Scottish Government making a request for a Section 30 order (parliamentary powers) to hold a referendum.[[89]](#cite_note-19_october-90) If that was rejected, it would ask the UK Supreme Court to adjudicate whether the Scottish Parliament could legislate for a referendum without that transfer of powers.[[89]](#cite_note-19_october-90) If that was also rejected, she wished to turn the next UK general election into a "de facto" referendum.[[89]](#cite_note-19_october-90)

A week later, Prime Minister Boris Johnson rejected the request for a Section 30 transfer of powers.[[90]](#cite_note-91) The Scottish Government lodged a case with the Supreme Court to determine whether the powers to hold a referendum are within the competence of the Scottish Parliament. A hearing took place on 11 and 12 October 2022,[[91]](#cite_note-:5-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93) and a month later the Supreme Court ruled that the Scottish Parliament does not have the power to legislate for an independence referendum.[[93]](#cite_note-:11-94)

Before the Supreme Court hearing Nicola Sturgeon said that, in the eventuality of the Court ruling that the Scottish Parliament did not have the authority to act unilaterally, the SNP would contest the next UK general election as a *de facto* referendum on independence.[[89]](#cite_note-19_october-90) This tactic has been previously used by [Irish](/wiki/1918_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Ireland "1918 United Kingdom general election in Ireland") and [Catalan](/wiki/2015_Catalan_regional_election "2015 Catalan regional election") nationalists.[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95) It has been criticised on the grounds that "elections and [...] referendums [...] are quite distinct" and that general elections "[are] often about a range of issues" and "it's not for a political party to dictate the terms of an election".[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95): 97 Prof Jim Gallagher, chair of Our Scottish Future, said that "whatever UK government emerges won't treat it as having been an independence referendum".[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95): 97

Ahead of the [2026 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2026_Scottish_Parliament_election "2026 Scottish Parliament election"), First Minister [John Swinney](/wiki/John_Swinney "John Swinney") said in September 2025 that the UK Government should commit to granting a second referendum on independence should the SNP win a majority at the election.[[95]](#cite_note-Swinney_sets_out_latest_plan_for_an_independence_referendum-96) Speaking at an event in Edinburgh, Swinney urged the population of Scotland to "come together and demand a say over our future".[[95]](#cite_note-Swinney_sets_out_latest_plan_for_an_independence_referendum-96) UK Prime Minister Starmer claimed that he does not foresee the SNP winning a majority at the Scottish Parliament election, and accused Swinney of engaging in "distraction politics".[[95]](#cite_note-Swinney_sets_out_latest_plan_for_an_independence_referendum-96) Since the 2014 referendum, unionist politicians have argued against a second referendum on the basis that the vote in 2014 was billed as a "once in a generation" opportunity, which Swinney disputes. In an interview with the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), he claimed that "by 2030 there would be one million young Scots eligible to vote who were too young to participate in the 2014 referendum", further commenting "that seems like a generation to me".[[95]](#cite_note-Swinney_sets_out_latest_plan_for_an_independence_referendum-96)

On September 4 2025, the Scottish Government published the *Your Right to Decide* paper, which sets out the governments prospectus on Scottish independence following a second referendum and establishes the view that "it is for the people of Scotland to decide on their constitutional future".[[96]](#cite_note-Your_Right_to_Decide-97) In the paper, the Scottish Government establishes the United Kingdom as a "voluntary union of nations", and therefore argues that Scotland should have the ability to decide whether to leave the voluntary union arrangement which established the [United Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "United Kingdom of Great Britain") following the signing of the [Treaty of Union 1707](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union_1707 "Treaty of Union 1707") by the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") and its [English counterpart](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England").[[96]](#cite_note-Your_Right_to_Decide-97)

## Legal position

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legal position")]

See also: [2014 Scottish independence referendum § Legality of a referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum#Legality_of_a_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum")

### Power to declare independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Power to declare independence")]

While in Northern Ireland, the system of devolution includes a provision for independence referendums, under the [Scottish devolution](/wiki/Scottish_devolution "Scottish devolution") framework, there is no equivalent provision. Therefore, Scottish independence would need to be enacted exceptionally by a competent authority. Due to the UK having no [codified constitution](/wiki/Uncodified_constitution "Uncodified constitution"), there is dispute over which authorities have competence to enact Scottish independence.[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95)

The UK Parliament retains [parliamentary sovereignty](/wiki/Parliamentary_sovereignty "Parliamentary sovereignty") over the United Kingdom as a whole.[[97]](#cite_note-98)[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) Under this principle, the UK Parliament could enact Scottish independence without the need for a referendum.[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95) In *AXA General Insurance Ltd and others v HM Advocate and others*, the Deputy President of the Supreme Court, [Lord Hope of Craighead](/wiki/Lord_Hope_of_Craighead "Lord Hope of Craighead"), stated that "the sovereignty of the Crown in Parliament [...] is the bedrock of the British constitution. Sovereignty remains with the United Kingdom Parliament."[[100]](#cite_note-101) However, the application of the principle of parliamentary sovereignty to Scotland has been disputed. In *[MacCormick v The Lord Advocate](/wiki/MacCormick_v_The_Lord_Advocate "MacCormick v The Lord Advocate")*, the [Lord President of the Court of Session](/wiki/Lord_President_of_the_Court_of_Session "Lord President of the Court of Session"), [Lord Cooper of Culross](/wiki/Lord_Cooper_of_Culross "Lord Cooper of Culross") stated *[obiter dicta](/wiki/Obiter_dictum "Obiter dictum")* that "the principle of the unlimited sovereignty of Parliament is a distinctively English principle which has no counterpart in Scottish Constitutional Law."[[101]](#cite_note-102) It has been suggested that the doctrine of [popular sovereignty](/wiki/Popular_sovereignty "Popular sovereignty"),[[102]](#cite_note-103) proclaimed in the 1320 [Declaration of Arbroath](/wiki/Declaration_of_Arbroath "Declaration of Arbroath") [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*], articulated by Scottish political thinkers like [George Buchanan](/wiki/George_Buchanan "George Buchanan") and reasserted by the [Claim of Right 1989](/wiki/Claim_of_Right_1989 "Claim of Right 1989") (signed by nearly every Labour and Liberal Democrat MP in Scotland at the time),[[103]](#cite_note-104)[[104]](#cite_note-105) is of greater relevance to Scotland.

Some lawyers have said that Parliament cannot repeal the Acts of Union because the *Treaty of Union* is a [treaty](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty") in international law, made by two no longer existing, independent states.[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95) The law scholar David Walker wrote that regardless of any amendment or repeal of the Acts, the *Treaty* would remain in force because Parliament cannot alter the terms of international treaties.[[105]](#cite_note-:6-106) Professors James Crawford and Alan Boyle write that it is unlikely the *Treaty of Union* can be considered a treaty, but rather, as Smith[[106]](#cite_note-107) wrote, it was a 'record of negotiations' and that the UK could not 'be bound by a treaty to which it was not party'.[[107]](#cite_note-108) This notion that Parliament cannot amend the terms of the Union's creation is challenged by the fact that the Acts have been successfully amended by the Parliament several times.[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95) David Walker writes that simply because Parliament purports to amend an Act does not mean it has done so.[[105]](#cite_note-:6-106) When HM Government ratifies a treaty, it does not however bring it into domestic law, it only creates certain rights or duties for the Government. An Act is only brought into domestic law by Parliament legislating thus. According to a 2017 Supreme Court ruling, ministers cannot make changes to UK constitutional arrangements without an Act of Parliament. There is no legal role for the Scottish Parliament or Government in treaties and under devolution, treaty-making is the sole responsibility of the UK Government.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

The legality of any UK constituent country attaining *de facto* independence or declaring unilateral independence outside the framework of British [constitutional convention](/wiki/Constitutional_conventions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Constitutional conventions of the United Kingdom") is debatable. Under [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law"), a unilateral declaration might satisfy the principle of the "[declarative theory of statehood](/wiki/Declarative_theory_of_statehood "Declarative theory of statehood")", but not the "[constitutive theory of statehood](/wiki/Constitutive_theory_of_statehood "Constitutive theory of statehood")". Some legal opinion following the [Supreme Court of Canada](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Canada "Supreme Court of Canada")'s [decision](/wiki/Reference_re_Secession_of_Quebec "Reference re Secession of Quebec") on what steps [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") would need to take to secede is that Scotland would be unable to unilaterally declare independence under international law if the UK Government permitted a referendum on an unambiguous question on secession.[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-111) The SNP have not sought a unilateral act, but rather state that a positive vote for independence in a referendum would have "enormous moral and political force... impossible for a future [Westminster] government to ignore",[[111]](#cite_note-112) and hence would give the Scottish Government a mandate to negotiate for the passage of an act of the UK Parliament providing for Scotland's secession, in which Westminster renounces its sovereignty over Scotland.[[112]](#cite_note-113)

The [United Nations Charter](/wiki/United_Nations_Charter "United Nations Charter") enshrines the right of peoples to self-determination, and the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") also guarantees peoples' right to change nationality; the UK is a signatory to both documents. Politicians in both the Scottish and UK parliaments have endorsed the right of the Scottish people to [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination"), including former UK Prime Ministers [John Major](/wiki/John_Major "John Major") and [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher").[[113]](#cite_note-114)

### Power to hold an independence referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Power to hold an independence referendum")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Official_Portrait_of_Dorothy_Bain_QC.png/250px-Official_Portrait_of_Dorothy_Bain_QC.png)](/wiki/File:Official_Portrait_of_Dorothy_Bain_QC.png)

The [Lord Advocate](/wiki/Lord_Advocate "Lord Advocate"), [Dorothy Bain](/wiki/Dorothy_Bain "Dorothy Bain"), argued the Scottish Government's case for the granting of a Section 30 order before the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") in October 2022

The issue of the Scottish Government's power to hold and the Scottish Parliament's competence to legislate for an independence referendum is a subject of intense debate both inside and outside Scotland. In November 2022 the UK Supreme Court gave a judgment that the Scottish Parliament does not have the power to legislate for an independence referendum.[[93]](#cite_note-:11-94) The [Scotland Act 1998](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998") reserved powers over "the Union of the Kingdoms of Scotland and England" to the UK Parliament. Any provision is outwith the competence of the Scottish Parliament if "it relates to reserved matters" under Section 29(2) of the Act. This formed the basis of the UK Supreme Court's judgment on the Scottish Parliament's competence to legislate on the matter.[[93]](#cite_note-:11-94)

To ensure the undisputed constitutional legality of the [2014 Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum"), the Scottish and UK Governments signed the [Edinburgh Agreement](/wiki/Edinburgh_Agreement_(2012) "Edinburgh Agreement (2012)") stating that both would accept and "continue to work together constructively in the light of the outcome [of the referendum], whatever it is, in the best interests of the people of Scotland and of the rest of the United Kingdom".[[114]](#cite_note-edin_ag-115) The agreement, with the subsequent approval of the UK Parliament, gave the Scottish Parliament special legal authority to hold an independence referendum before the end of 2014.[[114]](#cite_note-edin_ag-115)[[115]](#cite_note-116)

No such agreement has been reached in respect of a [second referendum](/wiki/Second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Second Scottish independence referendum"), throwing doubt over its legal status. In December 2019, Martin Keatings, a pro-independence independent candidate, sought a declarator to the Court of Session. However Lady Carmichael said the case lacked standing due to its hypothetical nature. Nevertheless, Mr Keatings brought an appeal forward in April 2021 as the Scottish Government had now published a bill,[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95) however this appeal was lost.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

With the publication of the draft [Independence Referendum Bill](/wiki/Proposed_second_Scottish_independence_referendum "Proposed second Scottish independence referendum")[[117]](#cite_note-118) on 22 March 2021[[118]](#cite_note-119) the question of legality was raised again. On 28 June 2022, Sturgeon proposed to hold a referendum in 2023, provided that the legality and constitutionality of the referendum is guaranteed.[[89]](#cite_note-19_october-90) The Scottish Government stated that their proposed referendum is "consultative, not self-executing". In UK law, a referendum can be consultative (such as the [Brexit referendum](/wiki/Brexit_referendum "Brexit referendum")) or determinative (such as the [AV referendum](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_Alternative_Vote_referendum "2011 United Kingdom Alternative Vote referendum"), which, if passed, would have automatically brought in provisions for the [Alternative Vote](/wiki/Instant-runoff_voting "Instant-runoff voting")).[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95) The Scotland Act does not explicitly state whether non-binding referendums on reserve powers were reserved.[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95) In the Scottish Government's written case published in July 2022, the Lord Advocate observed that "the Union of the Kingdoms of Scotland and England has been superseded as a matter of law and exists only as an historical fact. The [Scotland Act] would therefore reserve something that no longer exists."[[94]](#cite_note-Torr22-95)

The Scottish Government lodged a case with the Supreme Court to determine whether the powers to hold a referendum are within the competence of the Scottish Parliament, and a hearing took place on 11 and 12 October 2022.[[91]](#cite_note-:5-92) A month later, the UK Supreme Court gave a judgment that the Scottish Parliament does not have the power to hold an independence referendum because it relates to the Union of England and Scotland and the sovereignty of the UK Parliament, which are matters reserved to the UK Parliament.[[93]](#cite_note-:11-94)

## Issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Issues")]

### Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Culture")]

In 2014, the Scottish Government wrote that "[Scotland's] approach [to culture] has been, and will continue to be, distinct from that of Westminster". The Scottish Government position was that Scottish independence would give the Scottish Government more powers to encourage culture and creative sectors. In the event of independence, the Scottish Government planned to increase domestic creative production opportunities, such as by setting up a new [national broadcaster](/wiki/National_broadcaster "National broadcaster"), while maintaining access to current TV channels and with no additional cost to viewers and listeners.[[119]](#cite_note-scotland.gov.uk-120)

### Democracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Democracy")]

The concept of a democratic deficit is the most frequently invoked argument in favour of independence. England has a majority (84%) of the UK population. Thus, constituency results for Scotland rarely affect the outcome of general elections. From the 1960s onwards, average voting patterns in Scotland and England have diverged.[[120]](#cite_note-:0-121) Scotland has only elected a majority of governing MPs in three of the 11 UK general elections since [1979](/wiki/1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "1979 United Kingdom general election").[[121]](#cite_note-21election2-122) Devolution was intended to close this deficit,[[122]](#cite_note-123) but [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), which happened despite 62% of voters in Scotland voting against it,[[123]](#cite_note-124) has highlighted this concern.[[121]](#cite_note-21election2-122) The [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), which often forms the UK Government by winning general elections, has not won a plurality of seats in Scotland [since 1955](/wiki/1955_United_Kingdom_general_election "1955 United Kingdom general election").

Underpinning the democratic deficit argument is an assumption that Scotland is a nation with a right to [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination").[[120]](#cite_note-:0-121) Were Scotland independent, Scotland's population would possess full decision-making power in regard to the political affairs of its nation. [Alex Salmond](/wiki/Alex_Salmond "Alex Salmond") stated in a May 2012 launch that "the people who live in Scotland are best placed to make the decisions that affect Scotland."[[124]](#cite_note-125)

In January 2023, the UK Government blocked the [Gender Recognition Reform (Scotland) Bill](/wiki/Gender_Recognition_Reform_(Scotland)_Bill "Gender Recognition Reform (Scotland) Bill") from going to [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") after it passed the Scottish Parliament 86 to 39. The UK Government overruled the bill by using for the first ever time Section 35 powers under the Scotland Act; the justification for the move was that they believed it would impact equalities legislation, which is reserved to Westminster.[[125]](#cite_note-126) Nicola Sturgeon responded, arguing that the block had 'no grounds' and constituted a 'full-frontal attack on democracy'.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

### Nationality and citizenship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Nationality and citizenship")]

The United Kingdom is a plurinational, rather than multinational, state, where overlapping national identities exist. According to Keating, both Scottishness and Britishness can be understood as a national identity and one can hold one of them alone or both at the same time.[[127]](#cite_note-128)[[120]](#cite_note-:0-121) Many people in Scotland have multiple national identities. 59% in Scotland surveyed by the BBC in 2018 said they felt strongly British, though the figure is lower than the equivalent in Wales (79%) and England (82%).[[128]](#cite_note-129) However, the majority of Scots feel closer affinity to a Scottish, rather than a British national identity. In a 2021 survey, when asked about their national identity and only allowed to pick one option, 64% of Scottish residents identify as Scottish and 29% as British.[[129]](#cite_note-130) Furthermore, many in Scotland do not feel a national affinity to the UK at all. In a poll taken in early 2021 by Panelbase, a third of respondents in Scotland said they felt Scottish but not British.[[130]](#cite_note-:3-131)

A category of 'Scottish citizenship' does not currently exist, as [nationality law](/wiki/Nationality_law "Nationality law") is reserved to Westminster. In the event of independence, Scotland and the rest of the UK would need to set new citizenship laws to allocate [British](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law") and/or Scottish citizenship to existing British citizens and set out Scotland's new nationality laws. For the 2014 referendum, the Yes side said Scotland would tolerate dual citizenship. British citizens habitually resident in Scotland and Scottish-born British citizens elsewhere would have been able to become Scottish citizens automatically.[[131]](#cite_note-132) SNP MP [Pete Wishart](/wiki/Pete_Wishart "Pete Wishart") said in 2013 that Scots would 'of course' be able to keep a UK passport, but [Home Secretary Theresa May](/wiki/Theresa_May_as_Home_Secretary "Theresa May as Home Secretary") said Scots may not have that option.[[132]](#cite_note-133)

### Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Evening_view_across_Carlton_Hill_towards_the_Castle_%2813164472813%29.jpg/250px-Evening_view_across_Carlton_Hill_towards_the_Castle_%2813164472813%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Evening_view_across_Carlton_Hill_towards_the_Castle_(13164472813).jpg)

[Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), Scotland's capital city and [financial centre](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre"), the fourth largest financial hub in Europe[[133]](#cite_note-134)

Independence would mean a much greater change for business than devolution.[[134]](#cite_note-:10-135): 219 While Scottish nationalists generally assert that independence would not be economically disruptive, unionists and the UK government tend to assert the opposite.[[134]](#cite_note-:10-135): 231 Journalist Peter Jones writes that calculating the real economic impacts of independence (i.e. whether Scotland would be richer or poorer outwith the UK) is "an extremely hazard, if not impossible, task".[[135]](#cite_note-136)

If businesses concluded that independence would yield benefits, there could be positive economic effects. However, if businesses do not, they could postpone spending or investment plans or even leave Scotland entirely.[[134]](#cite_note-:10-135): 219 Uncertainty caused by independence referendums can also have negative implications for financial markets and the wider economy, depending on the likelihood that separation wins. This uncertainty has impacts not only in Scotland, but in the whole of the UK. Due to the relative size of Scotland in the UK, any negative economic effects would be felt worse in Scotland.[[134]](#cite_note-:10-135): 232

Economic modelling by the [Centre for Economic Performance](/wiki/Centre_for_Economic_Performance "Centre for Economic Performance") found that independence would hit Scotland's economy ‘two to three times’ harder than Brexit. According to their model, leaving the UK after Brexit could reduce Scottish income per capita between 6.5 per cent and 8.7 per cent, depending on trade barriers. Rejoining the EU would do little to mitigate the costs of Brexit, because the cost of removing trade barriers with the EU is outweighed by the cost of erecting trade barriers with the UK.[[136]](#cite_note-137) Scotland's largest trading partner is the rest of the UK, which accounts for £51.2 billion in exported goods and services, compared to only £16.1 billion to EU countries.[[137]](#cite_note-138) According to Nicola Sturgeon, a customs border with the rest of the UK would be needed.[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140) 42% of those in Scotland think it would be financially worse off outside the Union (compared to 36% who think it would be better).[[130]](#cite_note-:3-131)

#### Natural resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Natural resources")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/North_Sea_oil_rig.jpg/250px-North_Sea_oil_rig.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_Sea_oil_rig.jpg)

An [oil rig](/wiki/Oil_platform "Oil platform") in the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"). Oil production is centred in the waters off the Scottish northeast coast

The nationalist position is that only an independent Scotland can fully utilise and exploit its national resources, including North Sea oil and gas, for the benefit of its population.[[140]](#cite_note-141) According to the Scottish Government led by Alex Salmond in 2014, 64% of the EU's oil reserves existed in Scottish waters,[[141]](#cite_note-142) while the David Hume Institute think tank[[142]](#cite_note-143) estimated that "Scotland is sitting on oil and gas reserves worth up to £4 trillion".[[143]](#cite_note-144) Investment in and production from the North Sea oilfields dropped sharply after Conservative chancellor [George Osborne](/wiki/George_Osborne "George Osborne") imposed punitive taxes, undercutting the projected revenue an independent Scotland could claim.[[144]](#cite_note-145)

David Maddox, writing for *[The Scotsman](/wiki/The_Scotsman "The Scotsman")* in 2008, pointed to a future [Peak oil](/wiki/Peak_oil "Peak oil") decline in [North Sea oil](/wiki/North_Sea_oil "North Sea oil") revenue,[[145]](#cite_note-Maddox-146) within ten years oil revenue had fallen to 10% of the 2008 peak.[[146]](#cite_note-147) Some, such as [Ruth Davidson](/wiki/Ruth_Davidson "Ruth Davidson") of the [Scottish Conservatives](/wiki/Scottish_Conservatives "Scottish Conservatives"),[[147]](#cite_note-148) wish to reduce public spending and devolve more fiscal powers to the Scottish Parliament in order to address this issue within the broader framework of the Union.[[148]](#cite_note-149)[[149]](#cite_note-150)[[150]](#cite_note-151) Outlying regions such as [Orkney, Shetland and the Western Isles](/wiki/Constitutional_status_of_Orkney,_Shetland_and_the_Western_Isles "Constitutional status of Orkney, Shetland and the Western Isles") would be disadvantaged or deserve a greater share of oil revenue.[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154)[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-petition-156)[[156]](#cite_note-157)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

#### Public finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Public finances")]

An independent Scotland would have full autonomy over decisions on tax, spending and borrowing. Scotland would be able to issue [sovereign debt](/wiki/Government_debt "Government debt") and set fiscal limits.[[157]](#cite_note-:9-158) According to the Scottish Government, it is clear that Scotland currently pays its way within the UK.[[119]](#cite_note-scotland.gov.uk-120) The Scottish Government has proposed setting up a 'New Scotland Fund' to provide capital investment and boost growth. According to the charity *[Institute for Fiscal Studies](/wiki/Institute_for_Fiscal_Studies "Institute for Fiscal Studies")*, this fund would likely need to be funded by additional borrowing.[[158]](#cite_note-159)

According to an analysis by the *Financial Times*, an independent Scotland would have a large hole in its public finances. The paper's analysis suggests this would be due to the combined effect of lower than expected tax revenues, Brexit and the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), which have increased the country's budget deficit.[[159]](#cite_note-:1-160) A publication by the charity *Institute for Fiscal Studies* claims that in 2020-21 (during the COVID-19 pandemic), the national deficit of Scotland was between 22% and 25% of national income, though this is predicted to fall after the pandemic.[[160]](#cite_note-Phi21-161) The same publication claims that if it leaves the UK by the middle of the decade, Scotland would have a deficit of almost 10 per cent of GDP. If correct, this would mean Scotland would need to raise taxes or cut public spending[[160]](#cite_note-Phi21-161) by the equivalent of £1765 per person after independence to make the deficit sustainable.[[159]](#cite_note-:1-160) Short-term forecasts of public spending are inherently uncertain, but can still provide useful predictions.[[161]](#cite_note-162) A report published by the [Sustainable Growth Commission](/wiki/Sustainable_Growth_Commission "Sustainable Growth Commission") set up by the SNP to make recommendations on the economy of an independent Scotland stated that the deficit would need to be cut to 3 per cent of GDP.[[162]](#cite_note-163)

#### Currency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Currency")]

See also: [Irish pound § Second pound](/wiki/Irish_pound#Second_pound "Irish pound")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Museum_on_the_mound%2C_Edinburgh_%2841404375864%29.jpg/250px-Museum_on_the_mound%2C_Edinburgh_%2841404375864%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_on_the_mound,_Edinburgh_(41404375864).jpg)

The [Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Bank_of_Scotland "Bank of Scotland"), one of the world's oldest banks

Before the 2014 referendum, there were questions over the currency of an independent Scotland and whether it would continue to use the [Pound sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling"), adopt the [Euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"), or introduce a Scottish currency (often referred to as the "Scottish pound"[[163]](#cite_note-Green-164)). Uncertainty could be brought in the immediate aftermath of independence, particularly disagreement as to how Scotland would be treated in relation to the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), and the unlikelihood of the Bank of England accepting a [currency union](/wiki/Currency_union "Currency union") with an independent Scotland.[[164]](#cite_note-165)[[165]](#cite_note-166) The chairman of [HSBC](/wiki/HSBC "HSBC"), [Douglas Flint](/wiki/Douglas_Flint "Douglas Flint"), warned in August 2014 of uncertainty if there was an independent Scottish currency, or if Scotland joined the [Eurozone](/wiki/Eurozone "Eurozone"), which could result in [capital flight](/wiki/Capital_flight "Capital flight").[[166]](#cite_note-ScotlandHSBC-167) In 2018, the SNP suggested keeping the pound for a period after Scottish independence. Dame [DeAnne Julius](/wiki/DeAnne_Julius "DeAnne Julius"), a founding member of the Bank of England Monetary Policy Committee, has called this a "hugely risky experiment for Scotland".[[167]](#cite_note-168)

Nicola Sturgeon announced in October 2022 her intention for Scotland to continue using the pound sterling after independence. Monetary policy in this period would be set by the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"). Scotland would subsequently develop a central Scottish bank and would move to a Scottish pound "when the economic conditions were right".[[163]](#cite_note-Green-164)[[168]](#cite_note-169) The establishment of a Scottish pound would be at the decision of the Scottish Parliament, once the Scottish Central Bank has established credibility, foreign exchange reserves are sufficient and Scotland is fiscally sustainable. The Scottish pound could run on a fixed or floating exchange rate.[[157]](#cite_note-:9-158)

### Defence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Defence")]

See also: [Armed forces in Scotland](/wiki/Armed_forces_in_Scotland "Armed forces in Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Soldiers_Patrolling_in_Afghanistan_MOD_45154539.jpg/250px-Soldiers_Patrolling_in_Afghanistan_MOD_45154539.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soldiers_Patrolling_in_Afghanistan_MOD_45154539.jpg)

Soldiers of the [Royal Regiment of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Regiment_of_Scotland "Royal Regiment of Scotland"), the senior and only current Scottish line infantry regiment of the British Army Infantry

With control over defence and foreign policy, an independent Scotland could demand the removal of [Trident](/wiki/Trident_(UK_nuclear_programme) "Trident (UK nuclear programme)") nuclear weapons, which are based in the [Firth of Clyde](/wiki/Firth_of_Clyde "Firth of Clyde"). Nuclear disarmament is an issue long associated with the campaign for an independent Scotland, as outlined in the House of Commons Defence Committee's white paper "The future of the UK's strategic nuclear deterrent: the White Paper" of 2006–2007.[[169]](#cite_note-Committee2007-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171) The [Scottish Campaign for Nuclear Disarmament](/wiki/Scottish_Campaign_for_Nuclear_Disarmament "Scottish Campaign for Nuclear Disarmament") supports independence on this basis.[[171]](#cite_note-172)

Scottish voters are in favour of the Trident nuclear deterrent being in Scotland. Even in the event of a vote for independence, 45 per cent of Scots polled in 2022 think the nuclear deterrent should be maintained in Scotland, compared to 34 percent against.[[172]](#cite_note-173) While in the Union, 58% of Scots believe in the retention of the nuclear deterrent and only 20% definitely want it axed.[[173]](#cite_note-:7-174) In the event that Scottish independence meant the nuclear deterrent could no longer be in Scotland, there is a risk that the costs of relocation would make keeping a nuclear deterrent in the British Isles unfeasible.[[174]](#cite_note-lowyinstitute.org-175) In 2019, the NATO Secretary General said the maintenance of a British nuclear deterrent is important to NATO.[[175]](#cite_note-176) In 2022, 73% of Scots wanted an independent Scotland to be part of NATO and only 8% did not.[[176]](#cite_note-177)

Scotland is said to benefit from a collective defence force and an independent Scotland would weaken the UK's defence posture.[[174]](#cite_note-lowyinstitute.org-175) There are many UK defence installations in Scotland other than Trident and many Scottish people serve in the British Armed Forces. There are 12,000 service personnel in Scotland and a further 18,500 civilian jobs in the armed forces defence industry. It is expected Scotland would lose defence jobs. For example, [Lord West](/wiki/Alan_West,_Baron_West_of_Spithead "Alan West, Baron West of Spithead") said Scotland could lose 20,000 or 25,000 jobs.[[177]](#cite_note-178)

In 2014, a UK Government report stated that Scotland "plays an integral part in all aspects of the UK's defence". The report said the new Scottish Government would need to set up much defence infrastructure and services from scratch, and that existing defence assets in Scotland are well integrated into a UK-wide defence structure. According to the UK Government, it would be difficult for an independent Scotland to co-opt existing UK armed force units based or recruited in Scotland.[[178]](#cite_note-179)

### Foreign affairs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Foreign affairs")]

See also: [International relations of Scotland](/wiki/International_relations_of_Scotland "International relations of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Scotland_%28independent%29_in_Europe.png/250px-Scotland_%28independent%29_in_Europe.png)](/wiki/File:Scotland_(independent)_in_Europe.png)

Scotland in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe")
