Under the Scottish Government's 2014 plans for independence, Scotland would have applied to become a full and equal member of the United Nations, [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") and the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and many other international organisations.[[119]](#cite_note-scotland.gov.uk-120) With an [autonomous](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/autonomous "wikt:autonomous") voice in international politics, Scottish independence campaigners believe the nation's global influence would increase in regard to the defence of its national interests and the promotion of its values. Furthermore, Scottish embassies could be established globally to promote Scotland internationally, and to lobby other governments on the nation's behalf.[[119]](#cite_note-scotland.gov.uk-120)

During the 2014 referendum, a major argument against independence was that Scotland would be outside the EU.[[179]](#cite_note-180) Scotland is very supportive of EU membership, with 62% voting to remain in the [2016 EU referendum](/wiki/2016_EU_referendum "2016 EU referendum"). Since [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit"), many have called for a second independence referendum to have a chance to re-enter the EU.[[180]](#cite_note-181)

As part of the UK, Scotland is part of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the [G7](/wiki/G7 "G7"), and as a permanent member of the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council"). 82% of Scots believe that UK membership of these organisations is important.[[173]](#cite_note-:7-174) Opponents of further integration of the European Union have stated that independence, within Europe but outside the *[EU three](/wiki/EU_three "EU three")*, would mean that Scotland would be more marginalised because, as a relatively small independent country, Scotland would be unable to resist the demands of larger member states.[[181]](#cite_note-182)

### Links with the rest of the UK

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Links with the rest of the UK")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/British_Irish_Council_meeting_in_Glasgow.jpg/250px-British_Irish_Council_meeting_in_Glasgow.jpg)](/wiki/File:British_Irish_Council_meeting_in_Glasgow.jpg)

Scotland has been a member of the [British-Irish Council](/wiki/British-Irish_Council "British-Irish Council") since 1999, an intergovernmental organisation that aims to improve collaboration between its members

There are strong historical and contemporary ties between Scotland and the rest of the UK from the [Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation") and [Union of Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_Crowns "Union of Crowns"), to Scottish involvement in the growth and development of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire")[[182]](#cite_note-183) and contribution of the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment") and [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"). Contemporary popular culture is also shared, primarily through the prevalence of the English language. Almost half of the Scottish population have relatives in England.[[183]](#cite_note-184) At the time of the 2011 census, approximately 700,000 adults who were born in Scotland lived in the rest of the UK, while about 470,000 adults who were born elsewhere in the UK had moved to live in Scotland.[[184]](#cite_note-185) There are also significant economic links with the Scottish [military-industrial complex](/wiki/Military-industrial_complex "Military-industrial complex"),[[185]](#cite_note-186) and as argued by [David Cameron](/wiki/David_Cameron "David Cameron"), close links within the financial sector.[[186]](#cite_note-187)

In *Scotland's Future*, the Scottish Government wrote that an independent Scotland would "not affect the many other ties that bind Scotland to the other nations of the UK" and that there will still be a social union of family, history, culture and language within the British Isles.[[119]](#cite_note-scotland.gov.uk-120): 215 The UK Government wrote that a relationship of two sovereign states based on self-interest is 'profoundly differently' from being part of one state, and thus any co-operation would need to be in the interest of the rest of the UK, as well as Scotland.[[120]](#cite_note-:0-121) In the *[Building a New Scotland](/wiki/Building_a_New_Scotland "Building a New Scotland")* series of papers, published to support the argument for independence in a proposed second independence referendum, the Scottish Government advocates that "independence would mark a new phase in the evolution of Scotland’s relationships with the UK and Ireland. While the ‘social union’ of shared histories, sport, culture, languages, and family ties would continue as before, a renewed Scottish democracy would be a force for good across these islands". The Scottish Government advocates that an independent Scotland "will have close, mutually beneficial relationships across these islands on defence and security matters".[[187]](#cite_note-gov.scot-188)

The Scottish Government proposes that a reformed [British-Irish Council](/wiki/British-Irish_Council "British-Irish Council") would "provide a formal forum for managing some of these relationships, complementing regular bilateral discussions".[[187]](#cite_note-gov.scot-188)

Beginning close to the time of the 2014 referendum, it became a topic of discussion across British news outlets that [Doncaster](/wiki/Doncaster "Doncaster") in [South Yorkshire](/wiki/South_Yorkshire "South Yorkshire"), England has a claim to belonging to [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), by technicality. This originates from the [1136](/wiki/Treaty_of_Durham_(1136) "Treaty of Durham (1136)") and [1139](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Standard#Another_peace_agreement "Battle of the Standard") Treaties of Durham, in which [David I of Scotland](/wiki/David_I_of_Scotland "David I of Scotland") and his son [Henry](/wiki/Henry_of_Scotland "Henry of Scotland") were granted the Lordship of Doncaster by [Stephen of England](/wiki/Stephen_of_England "Stephen of England") to dissuade David from invading England. Historians observed that no known agreement in writing ever returned to Doncaster to England, unlike other territory offered from the north by Stephen, which is more commonly documented as being returned as a condition of the [1157 Treaty of Chester](/wiki/Malcolm_IV_of_Scotland#Malcolm_IV_and_Henry_II "Malcolm IV of Scotland").[[188]](#cite_note-189)[[189]](#cite_note-190)[[190]](#cite_note-191)

### Comparison to Brexit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Comparison to Brexit")]

The *Centre for Constitutional Change* stated during the 2016 EU referendum campaign that the "international relations aspect of the Brexit debate looks somewhat similar to the debate about Scottish independence".[[191]](#cite_note-192) There is no agreed process for Scottish independence and there would be no negotiations of the terms of independence before a positive referendum result.[[192]](#cite_note-193) [Ruth Davidson](/wiki/Ruth_Davidson "Ruth Davidson") has described "independence because of Brexit" as "amputating your foot because you've stubbed your toe".[[193]](#cite_note-194) The common use of the term Brexit has led some sources to describe Scottish independence as **Scexit**,[[194]](#cite_note-195)[[195]](#cite_note-196)[[196]](#cite_note-197)[[197]](#cite_note-198) a portmanteau of Scotland + exit.[[198]](#cite_note-199)

## Support for independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Support for independence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Scottish_independence_rally_2018_Largs.jpg/250px-Scottish_independence_rally_2018_Largs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottish_independence_rally_2018_Largs.jpg)

Pro-independence rally in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), 2018

Scottish independence is supported most prominently by the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party"), but other parties also support independence. Other pro-independence parties which have held representation in the Scottish Parliament or the UK Parliament include the [Scottish Greens](/wiki/Scottish_Greens "Scottish Greens"),[[199]](#cite_note-200) the [Alba Party](/wiki/Alba_Party "Alba Party") and the [Scottish Socialist Party](/wiki/Scottish_Socialist_Party "Scottish Socialist Party"). Other parties which support Scottish independence include the [Independence for Scotland Party](/wiki/Independence_for_Scotland_Party "Independence for Scotland Party"), the [Scottish Libertarian Party](/wiki/Scottish_Libertarian_Party "Scottish Libertarian Party"), and Sovereignty Scotland.[[200]](#cite_note-201) At the [2021 Scottish Parliament election](/wiki/2021_Scottish_Parliament_election "2021 Scottish Parliament election"), 72 of the 129 seats available were won by pro-independence parties (64 SNP and 8 Greens). The independence movement consists of many factions with varying political views. The SNP wants Scotland to keep the monarchy (see [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union")) and become an independent [Commonwealth realm](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm"), similar to Canada, Australia or New Zealand. All of the other aforementioned pro-independence parties want Scotland to become an independent republic. The SSP has led republican protests and authored the [Declaration of Calton Hill](/wiki/Declaration_of_Calton_Hill "Declaration of Calton Hill"), calling for an independent republic.[[201]](#cite_note-202)

The [Independence Convention](/wiki/Scottish_Independence_Convention "Scottish Independence Convention") was set up in 2005, seeking "Firstly, to create a forum for those of all political persuasions and none who support independence; and secondly, to be a national catalyst for Scottish independence."[[202]](#cite_note-203)[[203]](#cite_note-204) The [Scottish Republican Socialist Movement](/wiki/Scottish_Republican_Socialist_Movement "Scottish Republican Socialist Movement") is a Pan-Socialist independence movement that believes that Scotland should be made an independent republic. This movement has a Firebrand socialist ethos, however is not affiliated with the SSP or the Scottish Communist Party. It believes that a failure to become independent should lead to mass emigration elsewhere, or as put as a slogan "Independence or Desertion".

Apart from the official [Yes Scotland](/wiki/Yes_Scotland "Yes Scotland") campaign for independence in the 2014 referendum, other groups in support of independence were formed at that time. This included the [National Collective](/wiki/National_Collective "National Collective"), an artist-driven movement which describes itself as "an open and non-party political collaboration of talent focused on driving social and political change in Scotland through a variety of the arts".[[204]](#cite_note-205) It was responsible for organising a mock referendum held at the [University of Glasgow](/wiki/University_of_Glasgow "University of Glasgow") in February 2013.[[205]](#cite_note-206)[[206]](#cite_note-207) Another group, the [Radical Independence Campaign](/wiki/Radical_Independence_Campaign "Radical Independence Campaign"), described itself as "fighting for an independent Scotland that is for the millions not the millionaires". RIC was formed after the Radical Independence Conference 2012 in Glasgow, which was attended by at least 650 people and has been described as a "[bringing together of] the Scottish Greens, the Scottish Socialists, some of the more militant trade unionists, nuclear-disarmament campaigners and anti-monarchist republicans".[[207]](#cite_note-208)

During the 2014 referendum campaign, independence attracted little support from newspapers. The *[Sunday Herald](/wiki/Sunday_Herald "Sunday Herald")* was the only publication to endorse a "Yes" vote in the referendum.[[208]](#cite_note-national-209)[[209]](#cite_note-210) *[The National](/wiki/The_National_(Scotland) "The National (Scotland)")*, a daily newspaper supporting independence, was launched in November 2014, in wake of the Yes Scotland campaign's defeat.[[208]](#cite_note-national-209)

In October 2014, the lobby group [All Under One Banner](/wiki/All_Under_One_Banner "All Under One Banner") (AUOB) was formed. AUOB stages regular public marches in support of Scottish independence throughout Scotland.

Proponents of Brexit and Scotland's independence share relatively similar, but incompatible, objectives and difficulties.[[210]](#cite_note-211) Despite this, those who voted for Brexit in 2016 tend to be more unionist than those who voted to remain. The BBC reported that 39% of those who voted Leave in 2016 would vote Yes, while 59% of those who voted Remain would do the same.[[130]](#cite_note-:3-131)

On 14 October 2025, [Zack Polanski](/wiki/Zack_Polanski "Zack Polanski"), the [leader](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Leader of the Green Party of England and Wales") of the [Green Party of England and Wales](/wiki/Green_Party_of_England_and_Wales "Green Party of England and Wales") announced his support for both Scottish and [Welsh independence](/wiki/Welsh_independence "Welsh independence") with the [Scottish Greens](/wiki/Scottish_Greens "Scottish Greens") publishing a statement shortly after that "Every vote for Greens is vote for independence."[[211]](#cite_note-212)[[212]](#cite_note-213)

## Unionism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Unionism")]

Main article: [Unionism in Scotland](/wiki/Unionism_in_Scotland "Unionism in Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Scottish_Referendum_-_No_thanks_sign.jpg/250px-Scottish_Referendum_-_No_thanks_sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scottish_Referendum_-_No_thanks_sign.jpg)

A "No thanks" sign from the 2014 anti-independence referendum campaign

The [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)"), which all have seats in the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), are in favour of unionism. In 2012 they established the cross-party [Better Together](/wiki/Better_Together_(campaign) "Better Together (campaign)") campaign. Other parties that oppose Scottish independence include the [UK Independence Party](/wiki/UK_Independence_Party "UK Independence Party") (UKIP),[[213]](#cite_note-214)[[214]](#cite_note-scotsman2021-215) [All for Unity](/wiki/All_for_Unity "All for Unity"),[[214]](#cite_note-scotsman2021-215) [Reform UK](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK"),[[214]](#cite_note-scotsman2021-215) [Abolish the Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Abolish_the_Scottish_Parliament_Party "Abolish the Scottish Parliament Party"),[[214]](#cite_note-scotsman2021-215) the [British National Party](/wiki/British_National_Party "British National Party") (BNP),[[215]](#cite_note-216) [Britain First](/wiki/Britain_First "Britain First"),[[b]](#cite_note-217)[[216]](#cite_note-218) the [Scottish Unionist Party](/wiki/Scottish_Unionist_Party_(1986) "Scottish Unionist Party (1986)") (SUP), and the [Ulster unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") parties.

The campaign called "Scotland in Union" emerged after the 2014 independence referendum.[[217]](#cite_note-scotland_in_union-219) It has encouraged a positive outlook on unionism, tactical voting in elections and promoted the Union more generally.[[217]](#cite_note-scotland_in_union-219)[[218]](#cite_note-220)[[219]](#cite_note-221)'Scotland in Union' has been subject to criticism after records of its donor base were leaked, including many of the UK's wealthy elite, with major landowners, Lords, aristocrats and CEOs of companies all listed. Scotland in Union claims to be a “grassroots” campaign.[[220]](#cite_note-222)[[221]](#cite_note-223) In late 2017, a new group called "Unity UK" was formed.[[222]](#cite_note-unity_uk-224) Its supporters said that Unionists needed to be more supportive of [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") and were critical of Scotland in Union's "agnostic" stance on the issue.[[222]](#cite_note-unity_uk-224)

Many leaders of foreign nations expressed support for Unionism during the 2014 independence referendum. [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") expressed his support for a "strong, robust and united" UK.[[223]](#cite_note-225) Then-Swedish Foreign Minister and former prime minister [Carl Bildt](/wiki/Carl_Bildt "Carl Bildt") opposed the "[Balkanisation](/wiki/Balkanisation "Balkanisation")" of the British Isles.[[224]](#cite_note-226)

The [Orange Order](/wiki/Orange_Order "Orange Order"), a Protestant brotherhood with thousands of members [in Scotland](/wiki/Grand_Orange_Lodge_of_Scotland "Grand Orange Lodge of Scotland"), campaigned against Scottish independence,[[225]](#cite_note-227) and formed a campaign group called British Together.[[226]](#cite_note-228) In September 2014, it held a march of at least 15,000 Orangemen, [loyalist](/wiki/Ulster_loyalism "Ulster loyalism") bands and supporters from Scotland and across the UK;[[227]](#cite_note-229) described as the biggest pro-Union demonstration of the campaign.[[228]](#cite_note-230)

## Third option

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Third option")]

### Potential UK Confederation membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Potential UK Confederation membership")]

Main article: [Federalism in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Federalism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Federalism in the United Kingdom")

A Confederal UK has been proposed as a concept of [Constitutional reform in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Constitutional_reform_in_the_United_Kingdom "Constitutional reform in the United Kingdom"), in which the [countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom"); England, [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), as well as [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") become separate [sovereign groups or states](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") that pool certain key resources within a [confederal system](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation").[[229]](#cite_note-A_new_model_for_the_UK-231)

## Public opinion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Public opinion")]

Main article: [Opinion polling on Scottish independence](/wiki/Opinion_polling_on_Scottish_independence "Opinion polling on Scottish independence")

### Polling ahead of the 2014 referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Polling ahead of the 2014 referendum")]

Main article: [Opinion polling for the 2014 Scottish independence referendum](/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "Opinion polling for the 2014 Scottish independence referendum")

Many opinion polls were conducted about Scottish independence during the [2014 referendum](/wiki/2014_Scottish_independence_referendum "2014 Scottish independence referendum") campaign.[[c]](#cite_note-239) Professor [John Curtice](/wiki/John_Curtice "John Curtice") stated in January 2012 that polling had shown support for independence at between 32% and 38% of the Scottish population.[[237]](#cite_note-poll_research-240) This had fallen somewhat since the SNP were first elected to become the Scottish Government in 2007.[[237]](#cite_note-poll_research-240) The research also showed, however, that the proportion of the population strongly opposed to independence had also declined.[[237]](#cite_note-poll_research-240) Curtice stated in April 2014 that support for independence had increased since December 2013, although there was disagreement between the polling companies as to the true state of public opinion.[[238]](#cite_note-241) Polls in the run-up to the referendum vote showed a closing of the gap, with one [YouGov](/wiki/YouGov "YouGov") poll giving the Yes campaign a 51–49 lead. In the referendum Scotland voted against independence by 55.3% to 44.7%, with an overall turnout of 84.5%.[[8]](#cite_note-No-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Now-9)

### Polling since the 2014 referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Scottish_independence&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Polling since the 2014 referendum")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Flag_of_Scotland_with_Lion_Rampant.png/250px-Flag_of_Scotland_with_Lion_Rampant.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Scotland_with_Lion_Rampant.png)

Banner adopted by the Scottish Labour Hub to represent a new direction for the [Labour Party](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour") in Scotland.

Since six weeks after the 2014 referendum, opinion polls have asked how people would vote in a proposed second referendum.[[239]](#cite_note-242) Twenty-five polls were conducted in the year after the referendum, with seventeen of them having "No" as the predominant answer, seven having "Yes", and one having an equal proportion of respondents for each opinion.[[240]](#cite_note-243)

In the year from September 2016 to September 2017, 25 of 26 polls conducted showed "No" as the most popular answer and only one showed "Yes" as the most popular answer.[[241]](#cite_note-244) "No" continued to show a lead in opinion polls until July 2019, when one poll by [Michael Ashcroft](/wiki/Michael_Ashcroft "Michael Ashcroft") showed a narrow majority for "Yes".[[242]](#cite_note-ashcroft-245) Professor John Curtice said after this poll was released that there had recently been a swing towards "Yes", and that this was concentrated among people who had voted to "Remain" in the 2016 Brexit referendum.[[242]](#cite_note-ashcroft-245)

This pro-independence trend continued into 2020, as three polls in the early part of the year put "Yes" support at between 50% and 52%.[[243]](#cite_note-246) In October 2020, an [Ipsos MORI](/wiki/Ipsos_MORI "Ipsos MORI")/[STV News](/wiki/STV_News "STV News") poll saw support for independence at its highest ever level, with 58% saying they would vote "Yes".[[244]](#cite_note-247) As of December 2020, fifteen consecutive opinion polls had shown a lead for "Yes".[[245]](#cite_note-248) The run of polls showing a "Yes" lead continued into January 2021, although the average support for Yes was down by two percentage points compared to polls by the same companies in late 2020.[[246]](#cite_note-249) Polls conducted in early March 2021, following testimony by Alex Salmond and Nicola Sturgeon at a Holyrood parliamentary inquiry, showed narrow leads for "No".[[247]](#cite_note-250)

Support for independence in opinion polling depends upon the format of the question being asked. Polling company Survation have asked Scottish voters the question "Should Scotland remain in the United Kingdom or leave the United Kingdom?". Since 2018, none of these polls have shown a lead for Remain of less than 10%.[[248]](#cite_note-251) The rest of the UK generally supports Scotland remaining a part of the UK. YouGov polling between late 2019 and early 2022 shows that support for Scottish independence is at around 30%, while support for Scotland remaining in the UK is at around 45%.[[249]](#cite_note-252)

Following the Supreme Court Judgement of 23 November 2022, determining that the Scottish Government did not have the power to conduct a consultative independence referendum, the first five polls showed majority support for independence in the range of 51% - 56%. In a separate UK-wide poll, for the first time majority support (55%) was expressed across the UK for the right of the Scottish Government to hold an independence referendum.[[250]](#cite_note-253)
